explanation_used:
1. **Opening statement**: This neuron strongly responds to dense, descriptive text fragments that contain enumerations, appositives, or parenthetical details (such as lists of people and their roles, percentages and statistics, technical specifications, or quoted speech).

2. **Domain coverage**: The pattern appears across multiple languages (Catalan, English, German, Italian, Spanish, Turkish, and others) and spans news articles, cultural event descriptions, legal reports, scientific updates, and biographical narratives. High-activation examples often come from mid-paragraph text in these domains, featuring complex clauses and supplementary information.

3. **Contrast with low-activation examples**: Low-activation texts are typically concise leads, headlines, or datelines that introduce a topic without elaboration (e.g., location-based news openings or brief announcements). They lack the detailed subordinate clauses, lists, and parenthetical asides that characterize high-activation examples.

4. **Closing interpretation**: The neuron detects mid-sentence informational density and syntactic complexity, focusing on text that provides additional specifications, enumerations, or descriptive qualifiers within expository or narrative passages.

results.actual_text: hard-to-run chipmaking plant, and we can quickly figure out that America it’s slowly yet surely concentrating funds and important manufacturing capabilities for a highly technologized future
results.plain_text: Add the fact that Taiwan’s TSMC is putting $40 billion into a new and very hard-to-run chipmaking plant, and we can quickly figure out that America it’s slowly yet surely concentrating funds and important manufacturing capabilities for a highly technologized future. However, there still is something vital missing from all these major changes. Even though the U.S. will soon condition a portion of the EV tax credit on the origin of the minerals used in batteries, America still does not mine or refine a lot of lithium nor does it make enough of these very important energy storage units. That’s why we looked at some possibilities last year and showed you that exploiting the Thacker Pass area could help shorten the supply chain, secure a large quantity of lithium domestically, and lower the overall cost of EV batteries because of the mining, processing, and refining that can happen at home instead of overseas. South Korean, and one is Japanese. The potential is right there But it is estimated that by mining in the Thacker Pass area the U.S. could end up with the extraction of enough lithium carbonate to make over 950,000 batteries every 12 months for around 40 years. Apart from that, the entire zone has wide plateaus that can accommodate huge lithium pools. After word of what the U.S. lawmakers were doing got around, it did not take long before major companies started looking into Thacker Pass and the Canadians at Lithium Americas announced they would like to do the honors and started setting up shop. However, native tribes and environmental groups began fighting against the plans to transform the remote place found in Nevada that is affected by water scarcity. Trials can take a long time before a solution is reached, so the plaintiffs filed for an injunction. While the federal judge was pondering what the right decision is, General Motors (GM) didn’t waste any time and announced that it’s investing $650 million in Lithium Americas to secure the minerals needed for the batteries it puts into its current and future EVs. But the tribe representatives and the environmental activists lost a battle, not the entire conflict. According to Seattle Times, the 9th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals is still set to hear what the plaintiffs have to say about why the mining company should not be allowed to extract and process the lithium. American-sourced, American-made batteries (with Canadian help) They argue that the entire area could end up transformed beyond recognition and also claim that the wildlife would be affected. Both the activists and the tribes believe the air could end up polluted and the underground water resources might be contaminated. However, until justice says what’s ok and what’s not, Lithium Americans can resume operations. It’s a win for companies that want to make money from selling zero-tailpipe emission vehicles. As we said around a year ago, Thacker Pass might have to be sacrificed so we all can drive everywhere without burning gas. the first sodium-ion battery put into a real car. The best thing about this energy storage unit is that it does not require any lithium. All it needs is cheap sodium that can be obtained from electrolyzing table salt. Meanwhile, Europe and Australia are trying to figure out if the hydrogen-powered vehicle and green hydrogen could work for their green ambitions. For now, we’ll have to wait and see how things unfold. But one thing remains certain – everybody’s in a silent, yet very important race. Somebody is in the top spot, but that’s not guaranteed to last for much longer.

results.actual_text: re realizzati utilizzando l'immagine di Steamboat Willy finora protetta dal diritto d'autore. Un brevissimo
results.plain_text: Estratto da www.rainews.it    Non c'è voluto molto: poche ore dopo la 'liberazione' del 'primo Topolino' dalle maglie del copyright, è arrivato l'annuncio dei primi film dell'orrore realizzati utilizzando l'immagine di Steamboat Willy finora protetta dal diritto d'autore.    Un brevissimo trailer e il riassunto della trama hanno accompagnato il segnale che il topo del cartone […], diretto nel 1928 dallo stesso Walt Disney, è ormai alla mercé di quanti vogliano adattarlo a piacimento senza chiedere il permesso, pagare royalties e comunque rischiare azioni legali.    Questo perché il copyright sul breve filmato di animazione […] è scaduto alla mezzanotte di Capodanno sulla base di una legge voluta dalla stessa Disney che nel 1998 ha alzato da 75 a 95 anni i vincoli del diritto d'autore.    È la stessa sorte toccata a un altro franchise della Disney, quello dell'orsetto Winnie the Pooh, trasformato da un cineasta britannico in un mostro assassino che gira armato di mazza nella serie di film Winnie the Pooh: Blood and Honey. Il primo episodio è uscito su Peacock l'anno scorso e il sequel è in programma in febbraio.      Per Topolino c'è ora uno slasher, ancora senza nome, diretto da Steven Lamorte, la cui produzione partirà in primavera: il minaccioso topo è un sadico che tormenta i passeggeri di un traghetto, scrive Variety. Ha già un titolo invece Mickey's Mouse trap, il cui trailer è da qualche ora in rete con l'obiettivo di ottenere un distributore a marzo.    "È il 21/o compleanno di Alex, ma la ragazza è costretta a fare un turno di notte nella sala-giochi dove lavora. Gli amici hanno organizzato una improvvisata per festeggiarla, ma un killer in maschera vestito da Mickey Mouse decide di fare con loro un gioco di sua invenzione da cui lei deve sopravvivere", si legge nella sinossi.    E se i film non dovessero bastare, a ruota è uscito anche il trailer per un videogioco […] ispirato al 'primo Mickey' e intitolato Infestation 88. La Disney ha intanto messo le mani avanti: "Versioni più moderne di Topolino non rientrano nella scadenza del copyright e Mickey continuerà ad avere un ruolo guida per l'azienda […]”  winnie the pooh sangue e miele 1 winnie the pooh sangue e miele 4 topolino e minnie winnie the pooh sangue e miele 3 winnie the pooh sangue e miele 2 mickey mouse trap 6 mickey mouse trap 10 topolino steamboat willie2 mickey mouse trap 4 mickey mouse trap 5 mickey mouse trap 7 mickey mouse trap 2 mickey mouse trap 8

results.actual_text: the Conservative backbenchers, led largely by figures such as Tom Tugendhat in the China Research group, were leading the Sinosceptic argument in Parliament
results.plain_text: Up until this point, it seemed to be the Conservative backbenchers, led largely by figures such as Tom Tugendhat in the China Research group, were leading the Sinosceptic argument in Parliament.  But now, although maybe not in quantity but most definitely in influence, the Labour Party’s shadow cabinet appear to have taken the argument from underneath them.  Lisa Nandy yesterday revealed a Labour opposition to Chinese companies, controlled by the Chinese Communist Party (CCP), having influence in key British industries. Not only did it highlight an assertive, proactive shadow cabinet, but one which is in tune with much of the country.  According to the poll published this week, only 17 percent of voters are in support of allowing the Chinese firm Huawei a role in the construction of the 5G network in the UK. Additionally, 72 percent of voters are willing to pay more for telecoms and medical equipment if it were to make the country more resilient. The mood in the country is therefore being better reflected by the tone and proposals being put forward by the Labour Party, than those of the government.  The mood of the country has similarly changed regarding China’s treatment of Hong Kong and the position the UK should adopt. In 1990, a third (33 per cent) of those who had heard of the proposals to give British passport-holders in Hong Kong a permanent right to live in the UK, approved of them compared with 57 per cent who disapproved. This contrasts with 2020 when 64 per cent were in favour of the proposal, while only one in five was against.  The trajectory of public opinion is abundantly clear for all to see.  Although the Labour Party has generally been seen as the ‘doves’ and the Conservative Party as the ‘hawks’ in the response to China, Nandy’s intervention dramatically changes things. For no longer will the Conservative Party be able to coast over the tricky conversations surrounding China’s long-standing human rights abuses and increasingly autocratic, dictatorial treatment of Hong Kong.  There have been countless examples of the Conservative government over the past decade overlooking the morally repugnant behaviour of the CCP for the sake of economic or diplomatic cooperation: George Osborne’s 2015 visit to Western Xinjiang on a trade initiative (the region in which the CCP runs ‘re-education camps’ for approximately one million Uighur Muslims), the 2016 official visit of the Chinese President, Xi Jingping, to the UK, and Boris Johnson’s recent decision to allow the Chinese company Huawei access to 30% of the UK’s 5G network infrastructure (although with ‘safeguards’).  Due to the Labour Party’s previous ineffectiveness in scrutinising the government at, well...anything, there has been a lack of attention to the role and behaviour of China globally that will inevitably define the international arena for decades.  It is subsequently unsurprising therefore to see the shadow foreign secretary’s portrayal of the Conservative Party’s approach to China over the past decade as being one of ‘naivety.’  This is not to say that the Labour Party has historically occupied a controversy-free, morally commendable stance towards China - far from it. The treatment of protesters in London during the visit of President Jiang in 1999 was ironically indicative of Communist China rather than the supposed "bastion of free speech." Protesters were prevented from holding up Tibetan flags and banners in areas which the Chinese President would be passing - a restriction not imposed on the former prisoner of war protesters during the visit of the Japanese Emperor a month prior.  Although the debate between human rights and the protection of democracy versus economic and diplomatic strength is one that has reared its uncomfortable head consistently, the debate is far from concluded. In a healthy democracy there should be no ‘no-go areas,’ and concepts such as the untouchability of the Sino-Anglo relationship should be consistently reevaluated and challenged.  Lisa Nandy’s intervention on the subject will hopefully spark a wider national debate and further bring into question the largely toothless relationship the UK has maintained with China for some time. The Conservative Party should be worried, Labour have firmly parked on their lawn of Sino-scepticism, and the country is behind them.

results.actual_text: Pandemic exposed glaring inequality Deaths related to COVID-19 were more than twice
results.plain_text: Pandemic exposed glaring inequality Deaths related to COVID-19 were more than twice as high among black, Latino and Native American people as among whites in 2020, according to new research out from the National Cancer Institute. It’s only the latest reminder of the glaring inequity that plagues the U.S. healthcare system. Addressing that... www.southwestregionalpublishing.com

results.plain_text:
Snæbjörn er fæddur á Litla-Lambhaga í Skilmannahreppi 22. febrúar 1918, sonur Gísla Gíslasonar bónda og kennara og Þóru Sigurðardóttur. Snæbjörn var einn átta systkina sem sum hafa náð háum aldri. Kristín systir hans varð hundrað ára, Elísa 96 ára og Þórður 97 ára.  Snæbjörn starfaði við byggingavinnu en var lengstan sinn starfsaldur í fiskvinnslu hjá HB&Co. Síðastliðin átta ár hefur Snæbjörn búið á Hjúkrunarheimilinu Höfða á Akranesi.  Aðeins sex karlar á Íslandi eru yfir hundrað ára að því er fram kemur á vefsíðunni Langlífi. 
Snæbjörn Gíslason á Akranesi, Karl Sigurðsson á Ísafirði og Sigfús B. Sigurðsson í Reykjavík eru 103 ára, Karl Jónasson í Reykjavík er 102 ára, Jón Ólafur Ormsson í Reykjavík 101 árs og Þórarinn Gíslason í Reykjavík 100 ára. Í ársbyrjun voru 15 karlar 99 ára, fæddir 1922, og hafa aldrei verið fleiri á þeim aldri. Konurnar voru 22 og er hlutfall karla af heildinni mjög hátt eða 40% en yfirleitt hefur það verið 20-25%. Má því búast við að körlum hundrað ára eða eldri eigi eftir að fjölga umtalsvert þegar líða fer á árið.  Flestir hafa karlmenn verið fimmtán á sama tíma hér á landi, hundrað ára og eldri.
